role_summary: SNCC chairman 1966-1967; the person who introduced "Black Power" as a slogan into the movement at the June 1966 Meredith March in Mississippi; later all-African organizer who took the name Kwame Ture.

photo.alt: Stokely Carmichael (Kwame Ture) at a 1966 Mississippi press conference
photo.license: Public domain (U.S., published 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

ai_reading: Seven corpus voices discuss Carmichael: Judy Richardson (entry 69, SNCC documentation), Charles McLaurin (entry 17, Mississippi SNCC), Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (entry 53), Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (entry 83), Freddie Greene Biddle (entry 44), Juadine Henderson (entry 68, Mississippi SNCC), and Junius Williams (entry 72, Newark Black Power Conference). The cluster aligns with both Mississippi voter-registration (where Carmichael organized before the chairmanship) and Northern Black Power organizing (where his post-1966 work crossed into). His own memoir, edited by Ekwueme Thelwell (entry 34), is the catalog's primary scholarly source on this figure.
biographical_paragraph: Stokely Carmichael (Kwame Ture) is discussed in this corpus by 7 interviewees across the catalog (Judy Richardson (entry 69), Charles McLaurin (entry 17), Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson) (entry 53), Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (entry 83), Freddie Greene (Biddle) (entry 44), Juadine Henderson (entry 68), Junius Williams (entry 72)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats Stokely Carmichael (Kwame Ture) as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

interview_snippets.speaker: Phil Hutchings
interview_snippets.lead_in: Phil Hutchings, who joined the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) at Howard University and later became SNCC program secretary, recalls first encountering Carmichael as an undergraduate:
interview_snippets.quote: And then that grouping you had people like Stokely Carmichael, who came out of the Bronx, who I met in my freshman year.

interview_snippets.speaker: Courtland Cox
interview_snippets.lead_in: Courtland Cox, a fellow Howard NAG organizer, on the May 1966 Kingston Springs meeting where Carmichael replaced John Lewis as SNCC chairman, just after Carmichael returned from organizing in Lowndes County, Alabama:
interview_snippets.quote: He had a lot more energy than John in terms of that. He probably reflected the mood of the black community and a lot of younger people in SNCC.

interview_snippets.speaker: Judy Richardson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Judy Richardson, a SNCC staff member working on Northern fundraising in 1964, describes booking Carmichael and the SNCC Freedom Singers for a fundraiser before the term Black Power had entered the movement:
interview_snippets.quote: So I bring Stokely Carmichael, the SNCC Freedom Singers, so Bernice Johnson Reagon and Chuck Neblett and Rutha Mae Harris.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ruby Sales
interview_snippets.lead_in: Ruby Sales, who organized in Lowndes County, Alabama, recounts her first day registering voters there, when a sheriff drew a gun on Carmichael at the courthouse and threatened to shoot him:
interview_snippets.quote: And Stokely said to him, well, hell will be an integrated hell tonight. And to see someone be able to stand up before a gun, not crying, not afraid, not backing down, touched me deeply.

interview_snippets.speaker: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, a SNCC organizer in Alabama and Mississippi, places Carmichael's work after the 1965 Selma marches in Lowndes County, where the organizing produced the Lowndes County Freedom Organization (the first party to use the black panther as its ballot symbol):
interview_snippets.quote: But it was at this time that Stokely Carmichael was going off from the March and organizing people in Lowndes County, Alabama.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons
interview_snippets.lead_in: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons, who worked in SNCC's Atlanta Project, describes how the position paper on Black Power, often attributed to Carmichael alone, grew out of collective discussions she and others transcribed:
interview_snippets.quote: And that became a document that was later credited to Stokely Carmichael as being the author, but it was a group project that had authored what became the position paper on Black Power.

interview_snippets.speaker: Cleveland Sellers
interview_snippets.lead_in: Cleveland Sellers, SNCC program secretary alongside Carmichael, disputes the popular framing of a feud between Carmichael and Martin Luther King Jr., recalling King's 1967 invitation to Carmichael to join a meeting as King prepared to come out against the Vietnam War:
interview_snippets.quote: there was never a moment in which there was a antagonism between Stokely Carmichael and Dr. King. Matter of fact, when Dr. King came out in 67 with his position against the war in Vietnam, he called and asked Stokely Carmichael and I to come to that Sunday morning meeting

sources.title: Stokely Carmichael with Ekwueme Michael Thelwell, Ready for Revolution: The Life and Struggles of Stokely Carmichael (Kwame Ture)
